---
title: "Syllabus"
toc: true
---

> **Public version.** This page is the sanitized, public-facing summary
> of the syllabus. The full course syllabus — with section numbers,
> dates, room assignments, and institution-specific policies — lives in
> the LMS (Blackboard).

## What the course is about

This is a **2-hour weekly lab/studio course** that builds practical
fluency with the tools mathematicians and quantitative students
actually use. There are four required pillars:

1. **LaTeX** — typesetting mathematics, theorem and proof blocks,
   figures, citations, polished PDFs.
2. **R + RStudio** — data wrangling, visualization, simulation, and
   reporting.
3. **Technical workflow** — Quarto, file organization, reproducibility,
   and optional light use of Git/GitHub. Quarto is the connective
   tissue: most assignments are `.qmd` files rendered to HTML or PDF.
4. **Generative AI literacy** — responsible use, verification,
   disclosure, and critique. Grounded in published frameworks and
   peer-reviewed work — see the
   [AI reading spine](resources/ai-reading-spine.qmd).

**Optional bridge** (one homework, student's choice): a short
exploration of a second computational tool — SageMath / SageMathCell,
Python + SymPy, MATLAB / Octave, or a CAS option such as Maple or
WolframAlpha. See [resources/cas-options.qmd](resources/cas-options.qmd).

## Learning outcomes

By the end of the term, students will be able to:

- Typeset mathematics and produce polished PDFs using LaTeX.
- Import, wrangle, visualize, and summarize data in R / RStudio.
- Build reproducible reports in Quarto that combine prose,
  mathematics, and code.
- Use AI assistants productively — and **verify**, **disclose**, and
  **take responsibility** for what they produce.
- Manage files, projects, and reproducible workflows.

## Format and attendance

This course is organized around **weekly software tasks**, not
traditional lectures. Most scheduled meetings are **supported lab
sessions**: you may attend for help, troubleshooting, demonstrations,
and project work time. **You are responsible for weekly deliverables
whether or not you attend the optional lab session.**

A small number of **checkpoint meetings are required or strongly
expected**:

- **Week 1** — setup check.
- **Week 7** — LaTeX project sign-off / studio.
- **Week 11** — R project / mid-portfolio conference.
- **Week 15** — final portfolio, reflection, and/or conference.

In-person work happens in the Math Assistance Center (MAC) during
class time. Online students complete the same weekly modules.

See the [schedule](schedule.qmd) for the week-by-week topic map.

## Portfolio model

Across the semester, you will build a small **portfolio of
mathematical software artifacts**. This portfolio may live in a
**local folder, a private repository, or a public repository if you
choose**. Public posting of your work is encouraged when appropriate;
**it is not required** for assignment submission.

By the end of the term, your portfolio should include:

- A selection of weekly homework artifacts (mostly Quarto documents).
- A **LaTeX Project** — a polished short paper.
- An **R Project** — a Quarto-rendered data analysis or simulation report.
- One or two **AI module artifacts** — a debugging audit and a short
  reflection on responsible use.
- Optionally, a one-page **bridge mini-report** exploring a second
  computational tool.

## Data and software

You will use:

- **R + RStudio** with TinyTeX and Quarto. See
  [resources/software-setup.qmd](resources/software-setup.qmd) and
  [Lab 1](labs/lab01-install-stack.qmd).
- **Datasets** that come from one of four sources: **built-in R
  datasets**, **simulated data**, **public datasets with verifiable
  provenance and license**, or **instructor-approved student-selected
  datasets**. See [resources/data-guidelines.qmd](resources/data-guidelines.qmd).

## Final assessment

The final assessment is a **portfolio, a short reflection, and/or an
individual conference**. Specific formats — submitted portfolio, short
reflection, individual conference, optional short demo, recorded
walkthrough — will be announced via Blackboard.

The point is to **show what you built** and **reflect on what you
learned**. The packaging is flexible.

## Grading (provisional)

Assessment is **not finalized** for this version of the course. Work
in the course will be evaluated across four provisional categories:

- **LaTeX / mathematical writing**
- **R / computation, visualization, simulation, and reporting**
- **Technical workflow** — Quarto, file organization, reproducibility,
  optional light Git/GitHub
- **Generative AI literacy** — responsible use, verification,
  disclosure, and critique

Exact category weights and the best-of-N homework policy are finalized
each term and announced in Blackboard.

## AI policy (summary)

You are **encouraged** to use generative AI assistants — ChatGPT,
Claude, Copilot, etc. — as study partners, debugging helpers, and
drafting aids. You are **responsible** for the correctness,
originality, and citation of the work you submit.

Every submission where AI was used must include a short **AI Use
Note** with three labeled lines:

- **Tool** — which assistant (and approximate date/version if known).
- **Purpose** — what you used it for.
- **Verification** — how you checked, tested, revised, or validated
  the output.

The **verification** line is the point. Using AI is not enough; you
have to verify and take responsibility for the final work.

Full guidelines, examples, and the AI reading spine:

- [resources/ai-use-guidelines.qmd](resources/ai-use-guidelines.qmd)
- [resources/ai-reading-spine.qmd](resources/ai-reading-spine.qmd)

## Where things live

> Our **public course site** is the main home for notes, labs,
> assignment descriptions, examples, and resources. **Blackboard** is
> used for private and official course functions: assignment
> submission, grades, announcements, roster-specific information, and
> any section-specific due dates.
>
> In practice: **check the course site to learn what we are doing, and
> check Blackboard to submit work and view grades.**

## Policies (public summary)

- **Late work.** Weekly homework is accepted late with a credit cap;
  projects require an extension request before the due date. Exact
  numbers and dates live in Blackboard.
- **Academic integrity.** Standard institutional policy applies. Pair
  it with the [AI use guidelines](resources/ai-use-guidelines.qmd):
  cite tools, cite sources, do not submit work you cannot explain.
- **Accessibility, weather, university policies.** Linked from the
  Blackboard syllabus. Anything binding lives there.

If anything on this page conflicts with the Blackboard syllabus, the
Blackboard version wins.
